name: My insurance company is lowballing my settlement for a lifting injury—how do I fight it?

mainEntity.name: My insurance company is lowballing my settlement for a lifting injury—how do I fight it?

acceptedAnswer.text: You fight a low offer by obtaining an Independent Medical Examination (IME) to challenge the insurer's impairment rating and by documenting all future medical needs. Do not sign a settlement until an attorney reviews the long-term impact on your ability to work.
acceptedAnswer.description:
A "lowball" offer usually happens when the insurance company uses their own hand-picked doctor to give you a low impairment rating or says you are ready to go back to work before you are. You fight this by gathering counter-evidence, specifically an Independent Medical Examination (IME) from a doctor who isn't on the insurance company's payroll. 

You should never accept a settlement for a lifting injury until you know the full extent of your future medical needs.

Key Factors:
- Your current and future medical costs (surgery, PT, meds).
- The difference between the insurance company's impairment rating and your private doctor's.
- Your ability to return to your previous job.

Negotiation is a formal process involving a "demand package" that outlines why you deserve more. 

An attorney handles these negotiations and ensures that if the insurance company won't budge, your case is ready to be presented to a judge or administrative law judge.
